vocabulary.definition: A question that anticipates variability in the data collected to answer it
vocabulary.visual: "How tall are the players on the team?" → heights will vary

vocabulary.definition: Information collected by observation, measurement, or survey
vocabulary.visual: Player heights: 60 in, 63 in, 58 in, 65 in

vocabulary.definition: How spread out or different the values in a data set are
vocabulary.visual: Scores of 10, 12, 11 have low variability; 2, 15, 30 have high variability

vocabulary.definition: A method of collecting data by asking people questions
vocabulary.visual: "What is your favorite sport?" asked to 50 students

vocabulary.definition: The shape and spread of a data set
vocabulary.visual: Symmetric, skewed left, or skewed right

launch.narrative: You've just been hired as the data analyst for a youth basketball league. The head coach hands you a list of questions the staff wants to investigate about their players. Some questions will give you interesting data with variety, while others have just one fixed answer. Your job: figure out which questions are worth investigating with data.
launch.contextImage: Clipboard with player roster, basketball court in background
noticePrompts: Which questions could have many different answers?
Which questions have only one exact answer?
What makes a question good for collecting data?
wonderPrompts: Why would a coach care about questions with variability?
Could a question be reworded to become statistical?

explore.label: Sort each question into the correct category: Statistical Question or Not a Statistical Question.

cards.text: How many points does each player on the team score per game?
How many players are on the roster?
What is the free-throw percentage of each player?
What color are the team jerseys?
How many minutes does each player practice per week?
How many quarters are in a basketball game?
How far can each player throw the ball?
What is the height of each player on the team?

discourse.prompt: What pattern do you notice about statistical questions compared to non-statistical questions?
discourse.sentenceFrame: Statistical questions expect ___ in the answers because ___. Non-statistical questions have only ___ answer because ___.

approaching.stem: Which of the following is a statistical question?
choices: How many points did each player score this season?
How many points are scored for a free throw?
What sport does this team play?
How many halves are in a soccer game?
approaching.explanation: "How many points did each player score?" is statistical because different players will have different scores — there is variability in the data.

approaching.stem: Match each question to its type.

pairs.left: How tall is each swimmer on the team?
What color is the team mascot?
How many laps does each runner complete?
How many players are on a soccer team?

onLevel.stem: A coach asks: "How many hours does each athlete on the track team sleep the night before a meet?" Why is this a statistical question?
choices: Because the answers will vary from athlete to athlete
Because it has a numerical answer
onLevel.explanation: A statistical question anticipates variability. Different athletes sleep different amounts, so the data collected will vary.

onLevel.stem: Match each question with what makes it statistical or not.

pairs.left: How fast does each sprinter run the 100m?
How long is an Olympic pool?
How many goals did each player score this season?
How many innings are in a baseball game?

extending.title: Find the Reasoning Error

workedExample.label: Question
workedExample.work: How many touchdowns did the quarterback throw this season?

workedExample.label: Student says
workedExample.work: This is NOT a statistical question.

workedExample.label: Reason
workedExample.work: It only asks about one player, so there is no variability.

extending.correctWork: Even though it asks about one player, "this season" means multiple games. The number of touchdowns per game varies, so there IS variability in the data. This IS a statistical question.
hints: Think about whether the data collected would have different values across games.
The question asks about performance over a whole season — each game can have a different result.

extending.prompt: Two students measured their height every month for 5 months. Student A's MAD is 0.5 inches. Student B's MAD is 2.3 inches. What does this tell you?
extending.sentenceFrame: Student A's measurements are more ___ because ___. Student B's measurements ___ because ___.

connect.scenario: The school newspaper wants to write a story about the girls' soccer team. A reporter suggests asking: "How many goals has the team scored total?" The editor says they should ask: "How many goals did each player score this season?"
connect.promptQuestion: Which question is statistical? Why would the editor's question give the newspaper more interesting data?
connect.prompt: The reporter's question is ___ because ___. The editor's question is ___ because ___. The editor's question is better for a story because ___.

exitTicket.stem:
Which question is a statistical question?
A) How many students are in the school?
B) How many hours does each student in 6th grade exercise per week?
C) What time does school start?
D) How many days are in a week?
choices: B) How many hours does each student exercise per week?
A) How many students are in the school?
C) What time does school start?
D) How many days are in a week?
exitTicket.explanation: "How many hours does each student exercise per week?" is statistical because different students exercise different amounts — the data has variability.
